title: Pot-au-Feu
subtitle: French boiled beef and vegetables
excerpt: A whole piece of beef simmered for three hours with root vegetables, leek, and aromatics until both the broth and the meat are deeply flavoured. Served as two courses: the broth first, then the beef and vegetables.
sourceNotes: Pot-au-feu (pot on the fire) is one of France's oldest dishes and has been called the national dish. Documented in Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903), Elizabeth David, French Provincial Cooking (1960), and Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, and Simone Beck, Mastering the Art of French Cooking (1961).

content.text: Pot-au-feu is France's most generous dish. From a single pot emerge two meals: first the broth, clear and deeply flavoured, served in cups or bowls with thin slices of toasted bread; then the beef, sliced thick and soft, surrounded by the vegetables that cooked with it, with cornichons, Dijon mustard, and fleur de sel on the side.

The beef must be a cut that can withstand three hours of gentle simmering without falling apart. Brisket is the most forgiving; beef shin gives even more gelatin to the broth. Start in cold water and skim meticulously, the clarity of the broth depends on it.

items.name: Leek
items.prepNote: halved and tied with a string to hold together

items.name: Onion
items.prepNote: halved, charred in a dry pan

content.text: Put the beef in a large pot and cover with 4 litres of cold water. Bring very slowly to a simmer over medium heat. Skim off all foam assiduously as it rises to the surface, this takes 15 to 20 minutes and is not a step to rush. Once the surface is clean, add the charred onion, garlic, bay, thyme, peppercorns, and allspice.

Cook at a gentle simmer for 2 hours. Add the carrots, parsnips, turnips, celeriac, and leeks and continue simmering for 45 to 60 minutes until the vegetables are fully tender and the beef gives no resistance to a skewer. Season the broth with salt. Remove the beef and rest for 10 minutes.

Ladle the strained broth into bowls or cups first, with toasted bread and grated cheese if desired. Then serve the sliced beef and vegetables on a warm platter with Dijon mustard, cornichons, horseradish sauce, and coarse sea salt.

items.fix: Skim vigorously in the first 20 minutes; always simmer, never boil, cloudy broth is safe but lacks the clarity of a proper pot-au-feu
items.cause: Foam not skimmed at the beginning; or boiled hard

items.fix: Continue simmering until a fork can be inserted and withdrawn with no resistance; brisket can take 3.5 hours in some cases
items.cause: Brisket size varies; some pieces need longer
items.symptom: Beef is not tender after 2.5 hours

content.text: Pot-au-feu was, for centuries, the meal that kept the French peasantry alive. A piece of cheap beef simmered with whatever vegetables grew in the kitchen garden, producing two courses from one pot. The 19th-century food writer Brillat-Savarin called it 'the basis of French cooking'. The dish has close relatives across Europe: the German Tafelspitz, the Italian bollito misto, the Polish rosół. All the same idea. Everything slow-cooked in water, nothing wasted.
